mnemonics.title: Maximax vs Minimizer
mnemonics.content: MAX goes UP (high), MIN goes DOWN (low). MAX chooses the HIGHEST value, MIN chooses the LOWEST value. Think: MAX = 'More' = Higher scores

mnemonics.title: Alpha Beats Beta
mnemonics.content: Alpha (α) = A for 'Already' or 'Max's best guaranteed score so far' (starts at -∞). Beta (β) = B for 'Better' or 'Min's best option so far' (starts at +∞). When Alpha ≥ Beta, PRUNE!

mnemonics.title: Minimax Tree Traversal
mnemonics.content: Depth-First Search (DFS): Go DEEP first, leftmost branch completely before moving right. Maximizer at even depths (0,2,4...), Minimizer at odd depths (1,3,5...)

mnemonics.title: AB-PIVOT
mnemonics.content: A-B = P-R-U-N-E: Alpha Beta PRUNE When Alpha >= Beta. Remember: A ≥ B means 'Already Bad, No Need Exploring'

acronyms.usage: The algorithm alternates between Minimizer (player who wants minimum score) and Maximizer (player who wants maximum score). Use when analyzing zero-sum games like Chess, Tic-Tac-Toe.

acronyms.expansion: Best value MAX can guarantee so far
acronyms.usage: Passed down from parent MAX nodes. Initially -∞. Updated when finding a higher value. Used to prune MIN branches.

acronyms.expansion: Best value MIN can guarantee so far
acronyms.usage: Passed up from child MIN nodes. Initially +∞. Updated when finding a lower value. Used to prune MAX branches.
Minimax uses DFS to explore game tree completely in one branch before backtracking. More memory efficient than BFS for game trees.

visualTips.visualization: Imagine an inverted family tree. Root at top (current state). Branches split into possible moves. Leaves at bottom show final game outcomes. Levels alternate between MAX (circles) and MIN (squares).
Imagine cutting off dead branches in a real tree with pruning shears. When Alpha ≥ Beta, draw an 'X' through the remaining branches. The 'cut' happens where the condition is violated.
Think of water flowing UP from leaves. At MIN nodes, water passes through the LOWEST opening (bottleneck). At MAX nodes, water rises to the HIGHEST level. Final value emerges at root.
Draw two fences: Alpha (α) is a fence from the LEFT, Beta (β) is a fence from the RIGHT. When fences meet (α ≥ β), game over for that branch - no need to explore further.

analogies.analogy: Think of two negotiators: One always tries to get the HIGHEST price (MAX), the other wants the LOWEST price (MIN). Each anticipates the other's moves and counter-moves several steps ahead, just like in chess.

You're shopping for a laptop. You find one for ₹50,000 (beta). A salesman shows you one for ₹70,000. Since you already have a better option (≤50,000), you skip listening to all expensive alternatives - you PRUNE!
Think of chess players thinking: 'If I move my knight here, opponent might move queen there, then I can checkmate in 3 moves.' Each 'move' is one level deeper in the tree.
These are like the END of a maze. Once you reach a terminal state, you know your score/result. There's no further branching - the game is over.
Minimax is like playing both sides of a chess game optimally - you assume your opponent is equally smart and will make the BEST possible move against you, so you prepare for the WORST case.
